page.projects.title: Selected Work
project.plugin-system.title: Job Application Tracker — Chrome Extension
project.plugin-system.insight: 1-click job logging from any job board. Claude AI extracts the role, company, and details; Notion stores the record — no copy-pasting required.
project.plugin-system.problem: Tracking job applications manually is tedious and error-prone. Switching between a job board, a spreadsheet, and notes wastes several minutes per application — and details get lost. The goal was to reduce the friction to zero: one click from any listing page and the application is logged.
project.plugin-system.method: Built a Chrome Extension (Manifest V3) with a content script that reads the active job-listing page. Clicking the popup triggers a call to the Anthropic API (Claude Haiku) with the raw page text; the model returns a structured JSON of title, company, location, and role type. A second API call writes the parsed record to a Notion database via the Notion API, using Chrome Storage Sync to persist credentials between sessions.
project.plugin-system.finding: End-to-end logging time dropped from ~2 minutes to under 5 seconds. Claude's structured-output mode proved reliable even on highly variable job-board HTML — no custom parsers per site were needed. The Notion integration made it easy to add custom fields (priority, cover-letter status) without touching the extension code.
project.plugin-system.impact: The extension is installable from source in under two minutes and works on all major job boards (LinkedIn, Indeed, Glassdoor, Lever, Greenhouse). The architecture is intentionally minimal — swapping Notion for another storage target requires changing only one module.
plugin.feature.0.title: AI-powered extraction
plugin.feature.0.body: Sends the raw page text to Claude Haiku with a structured-output prompt. Returns job title, company, location, and role type as clean JSON — no per-site parser needed.
plugin.feature.1.title: One-click logging
plugin.feature.1.body: A single click in the popup confirms the extracted fields and writes the record to Notion. The whole flow takes under five seconds from any job listing page.
plugin.feature.2.title: Notion database integration
plugin.feature.2.body: Each application becomes a rich Notion page with structured properties. Add custom fields — priority, cover-letter status, follow-up date — without changing the extension.
plugin.feature.3.title: Works everywhere
plugin.feature.3.body: The content script runs on all job boards. LinkedIn, Indeed, Glassdoor, Lever, Greenhouse — if the role is on a webpage, the extension can log it.
page.about.title: About
page.about.subtitle: MSc Geospatial Sciences · UCL
page.about.intro: MSc Geospatial Sciences student at UCL, combining spatial analysis, data engineering, and visual communication to turn complex datasets into clear decisions — whether that's a routing algorithm, a market dashboard, or a quantitative study.
page.about.statement: My work spans the full analysis pipeline: writing SQL to extract and clean raw data, applying Python for modelling and automation, then designing the output in Tableau, R, or Streamlit so findings reach the people who need them. I'm drawn to problems where location is a variable — where proximity, density, or terrain changes the answer.
about.s0: End-to-end data pipelines — SQL extraction, Python transformation, and production-ready reporting
about.s1: Spatial analysis with QGIS, PostGIS, GeoPandas, and geographically weighted regression
about.s2: Dashboard and visual storytelling — Tableau, ggplot2, and interactive Streamlit tools
about.s3: Quantitative research design — led a 200-participant study analysed with t-tests and ANOVA
about.t0.title: MSc Geospatial Sciences
about.t0.body: Spatial databases, geocomputation, and machine learning. Modules in Python and R with distinction-level marks. Dissertation focused on spatial prediction methods.
about.t1.title: Data Analysis Intern
about.t1.body: Built SQL pipelines to extract and standardise sales metrics across five competitor companies, producing reusable tables that fed four recurring business reports.
about.t2.title: Tools & Dashboards
about.t2.body: Built a 60K-record London Airbnb dashboard in R and Tableau, and an elevation-aware evacuation routing tool in Python and Streamlit using OpenStreetMap data.
about.t3.title: Quantitative Study, n=200
about.t3.body: Designed and analysed a controlled study on gender differences in auditory HMI acceptance for autonomous vehicles. Applied t-tests and ANOVA; delivered an 8,000-word data-driven dissertation.

project.airbnb-london.title: London Airbnb Market Analysis
project.airbnb-london.insight: 60K+ listings across 33 neighbourhoods — spatial pricing, seasonal demand, and host concentration explored through R and Tableau.
project.airbnb-london.problem: Inside Airbnb publishes rich listing and calendar data for London, but raw data alone hides the spatial and seasonal patterns that matter to hosts, guests, and policymakers. The goal was to turn three messy CSVs into a coherent market picture.
project.airbnb-london.method: Cleaned and reshaped the listings, calendar, and reviews datasets in R — handling missing prices, normalising room types, and computing monthly occupancy proxies from calendar availability. Built four analytical layers: spatial price mapping by neighbourhood, seasonal demand trends, supply vs. occupancy dynamics, and top-10 host concentration. Visualised in a single multi-sheet Tableau dashboard.
project.airbnb-london.finding: Inner-east and central neighbourhoods command a consistent price premium. Occupancy peaks sharply in summer and around major events. A small cluster of multi-listing hosts accounts for a disproportionate share of supply — a pattern typical of platform economies.
project.airbnb-london.impact: The dashboard lets any stakeholder slice by neighbourhood, room type, and month in real time. Hosts can benchmark their pricing; analysts can identify demand windows; the Tableau workbook is fully reproducible from the Inside Airbnb source data.
olist.agent.intro: A conversational Claude-powered agent that runs the full analytical pipeline on demand. Ask it anything about churn, segments, or revenue — it selects and chains the right tools automatically.
olist.tool.0.title: Load Data
olist.tool.0.body: Merges 5 Olist CSVs (~100K transactions) into a single analytical dataframe with parsed timestamps and derived columns.
olist.tool.1.title: RFM Segmentation
olist.tool.1.body: Scores every customer on Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value, then assigns one of 6 business segments — Champions through Lost.
olist.tool.2.title: K-Means Clustering
olist.tool.2.body: Fits K-Means on log-scaled RFM features; validates k with silhouette score and auto-labels clusters (High-Value, Dormant, Recent, Mid-Tier).
olist.tool.3.title: Churn Prediction
olist.tool.3.body: Trains a Random Forest on 7 features; returns ROC-AUC, precision/recall, feature importances, and R$ revenue at risk from high-probability churners.
olist.tool.4.title: Cohort Retention
olist.tool.4.body: Builds a full cohort retention matrix and surfaces month-2 and month-3 retention rates with a plain-language intervention recommendation.
olist.tool.5.title: Pareto Analysis
olist.tool.5.body: Quantifies revenue concentration: what share do the top 10% and 20% of customers contribute, and what is the median vs. mean spend.
olist.tool.6.title: A/B Test Design
olist.tool.6.body: Computes required sample size and statistical power for a retention experiment given baseline rate, expected lift, α, and β.
project.olist-churn.title: Olist Customer Intelligence & Churn Prevention
project.olist-churn.insight: 97% of customers buy only once — an end-to-end ML pipeline to predict who's about to leave and quantify the cost of letting them go.
project.olist-churn.problem: Olist, Brazil's largest e-commerce marketplace, faces a severe retention crisis: over 97% of customers make a single purchase and never return, leaving substantial lifetime value unrealised. Acquisition-focused metrics masked this structural weakness entirely.
project.olist-churn.method: Built a full customer analytics pipeline on 100K+ transactions across 2016–2018. Steps: RFM segmentation with quintile scoring, K-Means clustering (silhouette-validated, k=4), cohort retention heatmaps, CLV modelling, and a Random Forest churn classifier trained on recency, frequency, monetary value, and delivery experience features. Paired with an interactive A/B test sample size calculator for experiment planning.
project.olist-churn.finding: Recency is the dominant churn predictor. K-Means surfaces 4 behaviorally distinct segments that sharpen the RFM rules. Top 20% of customers drive ~80% of revenue — yet most remain single-purchase buyers with untapped repeat potential. The Random Forest model achieves strong ROC-AUC, identifying at-risk customers before they silently lapse.
project.olist-churn.impact: Targeted retention of high-value at-risk segments projects a 3–5× ROI on campaign spend. The accompanying A/B test calculator operationalises the findings — letting the team size experiments rigorously before launch, turning a one-off analysis into a repeatable retention infrastructure.
